J1.basis: this SaaS mid-pivoted from "Lumina" (an AI meeting assistant) to **InboxZap** (an email-cleanup tool), but only the blog and metadata got updated — the homepage... still marketed the old meeting-assistant product
J1.rationale: The entrant noticed the meeting-assistant/inbox-cleanup mismatch but inverted its direction — concluding InboxZap email-cleanup is the CURRENT product and the live meeting-assistant homepage is stale, the opposite of the reality (live product = meeting assistant, GSC = legacy). It then built the entire plan around InboxZap-era content, landing squarely in the anchor_0 failure mode.

J2.basis: 12 blog posts pull ~14K impressions at position 40-60 with near-zero clicks... Merged into one comprehensive guide at /blog/post-3 with a 301 from /blog/post-7
J2.rationale: Only a single aggregate GSC figure is cited, never per-asset impressions/positions, and the posts are treated as current content to reconstruct wholesale rather than dispositioned as legacy; the lone explicit redirect is a cannibalization merge, not a data-grounded harvest/redirect/sunset walk of the inventory.

J3.basis: rewrote the homepage, nav, footer, and structured data around InboxZap; built out /features and /pricing
J3.rationale: Rather than balancing the tension, it doubled down entirely on inbox-cleanup content while overwriting the meeting-assistant positioning — falling fully into a failure mode with no articulated tension, equity-preservation reasoning, or sequenced transition.

J1.basis: this SaaS mid-pivoted from "Lumina" (an AI meeting assistant) to **InboxZap** (an email-cleanup tool), but only the blog and metadata got updated — the homepage... still marketed the old meeting-assistant product
J1.rationale: The entrant noticed the meeting-assistant/inbox-cleanup mismatch but inverted its direction — treating the live meeting-assistant product as stale leftover and the legacy inbox-cleanup demand as the current market, then rebuilt everything around InboxZap. That is precisely the anchor_0 misread: legacy demand mistaken for the product's target market.

J2.basis: post-3 and post-7 both targeted "bulk delete emails" near-identically. Merged into one comprehensive guide at /blog/post-3 with a 301 from /blog/post-7
J2.rationale: The only per-asset disposition is a single cannibalization-driven merge/301; the other 11 posts are blanket-reconstructed as current content with no asset-by-asset walk and no citation of real per-post impressions/positions from the export (only a generic ~14K aggregate).

J3.basis: reconstructed all 12 posts as a proper app/blog/[slug] route... built out /features and /pricing... around InboxZap
J3.rationale: Falls fully into anchor_0's first failure mode: doubles down on the inbox-cleanup cluster as the growth engine while treating the actual live meeting-assistant product as the thing to overwrite — no tension articulated, no meeting-assistant cluster, no bridging of equity toward the current positioning.

J1.basis: this SaaS mid-pivoted from "Lumina" (an AI meeting assistant) to InboxZap (an email-cleanup tool), but only the blog and metadata got updated — the homepage... still marketed the old meeting-assistant product
J1.rationale: The entrant inverted the pivot: it treats the meeting-assistant live site as the stale legacy and the inbox-cleanup GSC/blog as the current product, then rebuilds the entire site around InboxZap — the exact anchor_0 behavior of doubling down on inbox-cleanup content.

J2.basis: Keyword cannibalization: post-3 and post-7 both targeted "bulk delete emails"... Merged into one comprehensive guide at /blog/post-3 with a 301 from /blog/post-7
J2.rationale: There is exactly one redirect, and it is a cannibalization merge rather than a migration disposition; the other 11 posts are simply reconstructed wholesale with no per-asset harvest/sunset calls and zero citation of actual impressions or positions from the GSC export.

J3.basis: rewrote the homepage, nav, footer, and structured data around InboxZap; built out /features and /pricing
J3.rationale: The entrant falls squarely into a failure mode — it abandons the real current positioning (meeting assistant) and pours all effort into ranking legacy inbox-cleanup content harder, with no articulation of the tension and no bridge; there is no defensible middle path, just a full commitment to the wrong side.
